Detroit Free Press investigative reporting 2015–2016

netherlands-toeslagenaffaire.overviewTitle: The algorithm that brought down a government
netherlands-toeslagenaffaire.overviewNote: The Dutch childcare benefits system flagged 26,000 families as fraudulent using nationality as a risk factor. The cabinet resigned when the full scale became public. It is the only AI governance failure in this dataset to trigger a government collapse.
overview: The Netherlands Toeslagenaffaire (Allowance Affair) was a systematic failure of the Dutch Tax and Customs Administration (Belastingdienst) to administer childcare benefit subsidies lawfully. Beginning in the early 2010s, the Administration deployed a risk-scoring algorithm to detect childcare benefit fraud. The algorithm used nationality — specifically, whether an applicant had dual nationality — as a risk factor. Families flagged as high-risk were subjected to intensive review and in many cases had their entire benefit entitlement clawed back, including benefits that had been correctly paid.

Approximately 26,000 families were wrongly assessed as fraudulent. Many were required to repay tens of thousands of euros immediately, with no adequate notice, no meaningful opportunity to challenge the determination, and no explanation of how the figure was calculated. Families lost their homes, their relationships broke down, and children were taken into care as a direct consequence of benefit withdrawal. A parliamentary inquiry found that the Administration had engaged in institutional dishonesty — suppressing information about the algorithm's functioning, denying that nationality was used as a criterion even after it was documented to have been, and failing to disclose adverse findings to Parliament for years. The Rutte III cabinet resigned in January 2021 following publication of the inquiry report. This is the only case in the HSRL dataset in which a governance failure in an automated system directly triggered a government resignation.
tapeItems: ONLY AI CASE TO BRING DOWN A GOVERNMENT

timeline.event: Risk-scoring algorithm deployed — nationality used as criterion
timeline.detail: The Belastingdienst deploys automated risk-scoring for childcare benefit fraud detection. Nationality — specifically dual nationality — is incorporated as a risk factor. No independent validation of the criterion's legal basis or accuracy is commissioned. No named individual is documented as accountable for the lawfulness of using nationality as a fraud predictor.

timeline.event: 26,000 families assessed as fraudulent — repayments demanded
timeline.detail: Families flagged by the algorithm receive immediate repayment demands for their entire benefit entitlement, including correctly paid amounts. Many are given no explanation of the basis for the demand. The administrative appeals process is effectively inaccessible. Families lose homes, marriages break down, children are taken into care. The Administration internally documents complaints at scale but does not treat the complaint pattern as a signal that the algorithm is producing systematically incorrect outputs.

timeline.event: Internal documentation of nationality criterion — suppressed from Parliament
timeline.detail: Internal documents confirm that nationality was used as a risk factor. Ministers and senior officials are aware of this. Parliament is not informed. Parliamentary questions about the algorithm's criteria receive answers that omit the nationality criterion. This is the documented suppression event that confirms C5 in this case record.

timeline.event: Parliamentary questions expose the scale — investigation opens
timeline.detail: Opposition MPs and journalists force disclosure of the scale of wrongful assessments. The State Secretary for Finance acknowledges the scale of the problem publicly for the first time. A formal parliamentary inquiry opens.

timeline.event: Parliamentary inquiry report — 'unprecedented injustice'
timeline.detail: The parliamentary inquiry report concludes that the Administration committed 'unprecedented injustice' against tens of thousands of families. The report finds evidence of institutional dishonesty: the nationality criterion was actively concealed from Parliament, adverse findings were suppressed, and families were denied fair process. The report is the evidentiary foundation for both the cabinet resignation and subsequent criminal referrals.

timeline.event: Rutte III cabinet resigns
timeline.detail: The entire Dutch cabinet resigns following publication of the inquiry report. This is unprecedented in Dutch political history and is the only case in the HSRL dataset in which a governance failure in an automated system directly caused a government to fall. The accountability arc from algorithm deployment to cabinet resignation is approximately 8 years.

timeline.event: Remediation ongoing — compensation incomplete
timeline.detail: The Dutch government establishes a remediation process. Compensation payments begin but the process is slow and contested. Many affected families report that the remediation process replicates the inaccessibility of the original appeals process. Criminal referrals against individual officials are ongoing. The accountability arc remains incomplete.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No individual at the Belastingdienst was documented as the named accountable owner for the lawfulness of the nationality criterion in the fraud-scoring algorithm. The decision to use nationality as a risk factor was an operational choice that entered the algorithm without a named official signing off on its legal basis. When the criterion was later found to have been used, responsibility diffused across the tax administration's leadership structure without a single named owner.

The parliamentary inquiry found that ministers received briefings that omitted the nationality criterion even when direct questions were asked. The absence of a named accountable owner for the algorithm's criteria meant that when the question of lawfulness was eventually raised, there was no individual whose documented role required them to have answered it before deployment.
conditions.quote: The question of whether nationality was a lawful risk criterion had no named owner — and so it was never seriously asked.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: The fraud-scoring algorithm was never independently validated for legal compliance — specifically, for whether using nationality as a risk factor was consistent with Dutch anti-discrimination law and EU law. The Administration's own legal team did not conduct or commission an independent review of the criteria before deployment.

The C2 failure here is a legal validation failure rather than a technical accuracy failure. The algorithm may have functioned as designed — the problem was that what it was designed to do was unlawful. Independent legal validation of the algorithm's criteria before deployment would have identified the nationality criterion as legally problematic immediately.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: The Administration received complaints from affected families at significant volume throughout the harm window. No structured monitoring mechanism connected the complaint rate, the appeal reversal rate, or the pattern of wrongful repayment demands to a review of whether the algorithm's criteria were producing lawful outputs.

The same failure pattern as MiDAS and Robodebt: the data existed to identify the problem within the first year of operation. The monitoring mechanism that would have required someone to look at it and act on it did not exist. Each complaint was treated as an individual dispute rather than as a signal about systemic algorithmic failure.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol defined what happened when families challenged the basis for their debt determination. The default — that the algorithmic determination was correct and the family bore the burden of disproving it — was never formally adopted as a governance position. It was the operational default that emerged from C4 absence.

A pre-established accountability protocol would have required the Administration to define in advance: at what point does the volume of successful appeals trigger a methodology review? That question was never answered before deployment. The Administration continued issuing determinations for years after the appeal reversal rate signalled that the methodology was systematically incorrect.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: The parliamentary inquiry confirmed that the nationality criterion was actively concealed from Parliament. Ministers received briefings that omitted the criterion. Parliamentary questions about the algorithm's risk factors received answers that did not disclose nationality. Internal documents confirming the criterion's use were not disclosed when they should have been.

This is the defining feature of the Toeslagenaffaire as a suppression case: it is not just that information was not volunteered — it is that direct parliamentary questions received answers that omitted material information. The inquiry characterised this as institutional dishonesty. The cabinet resigned on the basis of this finding. The suppression pattern is confirmed at the highest available level of institutional accountability.

tags: L-09 Compliance artifacts substitute for safety
laundering.desc: L-03: Nationality criterion omitted from Parliamentary answers despite internal documentation. L-09: The Administration's own internal review process was cited as evidence of due diligence while the adverse findings from that process were suppressed. L-16: Families required to disprove algorithmic determinations using records many no longer held.

prevention.title: A named official accountable for the lawfulness of each algorithmic risk criterion
prevention.desc: Before the nationality criterion entered the algorithm, one named official should have been documented as accountable for confirming it had a valid legal basis. That person's name on record before deployment creates the accountability anchor. When the criterion was later challenged, there would have been a named individual whose documented responsibility required them to have answered the legal question before it was used.

prevention.title: Independent legal validation of all risk criteria before deployment
prevention.desc: The nationality criterion's legal basis — whether it was consistent with Dutch anti-discrimination law and EU equality requirements — should have been independently reviewed before use. An independent legal reviewer would have identified the problem immediately. The review would have cost a fraction of the eventual €1 billion+ remediation.

prevention.title: An appeal reversal rate threshold that triggered algorithm review
prevention.desc: If the Administration had defined in advance that any quarter in which more than X% of benefit repayment demands are successfully appealed triggers a mandatory methodology review, the signal would have been visible within the first year. The appeal data existed throughout the harm window. The monitoring mechanism did not.

prevention.title: A mandatory repayment suspension when a family demonstrates they cannot have committed the fraud alleged
prevention.desc: The Administration demanded immediate repayment of entire benefit entitlements, including correctly paid amounts, without adequate process. A pre-established protocol requiring suspension of repayment demands pending independent case review when a family provides evidence inconsistent with the fraud determination would have broken the automatic harm pathway.

prevention.title: A statutory duty to disclose algorithm criteria to Parliament on request
prevention.desc: The suppression in the Toeslagenaffaire operated through omission in Parliamentary briefings. A statutory duty — requiring ministers to disclose all algorithmic risk criteria used in benefit administration when asked by Parliament — would have made the concealment of the nationality criterion a formal legal breach rather than a political judgment call. The concealment was sustained because it was discretionary. Mandatory disclosure removes the discretion.

comparables.name: Australia Robodebt
comparables.desc: Strongest structural comparator: automated welfare debt recovery, reversal of burden of proof, sustained suppression confirmed by Royal Commission, comparable justice lag. The Rutte III cabinet resignation and the Australian Royal Commission are the two clearest examples of governance failure reaching the highest level of institutional accountability.

comparables.name: Michigan MiDAS
comparables.desc: Direct US domestic comparator: automated fraud detection in welfare benefits, false positive rates documented, state awareness and continued operation. MiDAS operated at smaller scale with faster shutdown. Together, Toeslagenaffaire and MiDAS bracket the international spread of this failure pattern in benefits administration.

comparables.name: Norway NAV
comparables.desc: Direct resilient comparator in the same domain and region. Norway's welfare system self-identified a systematic error in benefit calculations, disclosed it publicly, and remediated within 18 months without suppression. The structural contrast is exact: same domain, adjacent geography, radically different outcome because governance conditions were present.

arkansas-medicaid-algorithm.overviewTitle: A federal court found the algorithm itself was the due process violation
arkansas-medicaid-algorithm.overviewNote: Arkansas cut Medicaid home care hours for thousands of disabled adults using an algorithm no one could explain. The Eighth Circuit ruled that the inability to explain the algorithm's outputs violated the Constitution.
overview: In 2016, Arkansas replaced its manual Medicaid home care assessment process with an algorithmic system developed by vendor Optum to calculate the number of personal care hours disabled and elderly Medicaid recipients would receive. The algorithm produced assessments that dramatically cut hours for many recipients — in some cases reducing allocations from over 50 hours per week to under 10 — without explanation. Recipients who challenged the cuts were told only that the algorithm had determined the hours. No caseworker could explain how the figure was reached.

Multiple affected individuals sued. In Ledgerwood v. Jester and the companion case involving Ledgerwood's attorney, the Eighth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in 2019 that Arkansas's failure to provide an explanation for the algorithm's outputs violated the due process rights of Medicaid recipients. The court held that notice of a benefit cut must include a meaningful explanation of the reasons for the decision — and that 'the algorithm determined it' is not a meaningful explanation. This is the foundational federal court ruling on algorithmic due process in US administrative law and is the case most likely to be cited in any federal investigation or litigation involving automated benefit determination.
tapeItems: FOUNDATIONAL US ALGORITHMIC DUE PROCESS CASE

timeline.event: Arkansas deploys Optum algorithm for Medicaid home care assessment
timeline.detail: Arkansas replaces manual personal care assessments with an algorithm developed by Optum. The algorithm calculates the number of home care hours each Medicaid recipient requires. No independent validation of the algorithm's accuracy for this population is commissioned before deployment. No named state official is documented as accountable for the accuracy of the algorithm's outputs.

timeline.event: Hours cut dramatically — recipients cannot get explanation
timeline.detail: Thousands of disabled and elderly Medicaid recipients receive notifications that their home care hours have been cut, in many cases severely. When recipients and their advocates ask why the hours were cut, caseworkers cannot explain the basis for the algorithm's determination. The notice provided states only the new hour allocation, not the reasoning behind it.

timeline.event: Legal challenges filed — state defends algorithm as proprietary
timeline.detail: Affected individuals, represented by the National Health Law Program and disability rights advocates, file suit in federal court. Arkansas's initial position is that the algorithm's methodology is proprietary and need not be disclosed. This position — that the basis for a government benefit determination can be withheld as a trade secret — is the C5 mechanism in this case.

timeline.event: Eighth Circuit rules algorithm-only explanation violates due process
timeline.detail: The Eighth Circuit Court of Appeals rules in Ledgerwood v. Jester that Arkansas's failure to provide an explanation for the algorithm's outputs violated the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The court holds that a Medicaid recipient is entitled to a meaningful explanation of why their benefits were cut — and that the algorithm's output, without explanation of the reasoning behind it, does not satisfy this requirement.

timeline.event: Remediation ordered — systemic reform incomplete
timeline.detail: Arkansas is required to provide meaningful explanations for benefit determinations and to revise its appeals process. The underlying algorithm continues to be used in modified form. The question of whether the algorithm produces accurate and equitable assessments — the C2 question — has not been independently answered. The case is the most-cited US precedent for algorithmic due process but the systemic reform it required remains contested.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named Arkansas state official was documented as accountable for the accuracy or legal compliance of the Optum algorithm's outputs. The algorithm was procured from a vendor and deployed by the state's Medicaid agency without a named official whose role required them to confirm that the algorithm's outputs would satisfy due process requirements.

The structure of the failure — a state agency deploying a vendor's proprietary algorithm with no named state official owning the accuracy question — is the operational definition of C1 absence in government algorithmic procurement. The vendor owns the algorithm; the state pays for it; no one owns responsibility for whether it produces lawful outputs for this specific population.
conditions.quote: No state official could explain the algorithm — because no state official was accountable for understanding it.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: The Optum algorithm was not independently validated for accuracy against the Arkansas Medicaid population before deployment. Medicaid home care needs for disabled adults vary significantly by individual medical history, living situation, and support network. An algorithm trained on a different population, or using proxy variables that don't accurately reflect actual care needs, will produce systematically inaccurate outputs for this population.

The federal court ruling illuminates the C2 failure precisely: the court found that the algorithm's outputs could not be explained. An algorithm whose outputs cannot be explained in terms of the inputs that produced them is, by definition, an algorithm that has not been independently validated — because validation requires being able to test whether the outputs follow from the inputs in a documented, auditable way.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring mechanism tracked whether the algorithm's hour allocations corresponded to the actual care needs of recipients. The basic monitoring question — do recipients whose hours were cut by the algorithm experience adverse health outcomes at higher rates than those whose hours were maintained? — was never systematically asked.

The appeal volume provided a monitoring signal. Thousands of recipients challenged their determinations. No mechanism connected the appeal rate to a review of whether the algorithm was producing accurate outputs. Each appeal was processed individually without triggering a systematic review of the methodology.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol required Arkansas to suspend algorithmic hour reductions pending explanation when a recipient requested one. The default — reduce hours per algorithm, place burden of appeal on recipient — operated without a pre-established checkpoint requiring the state to confirm the reduction was explainable before implementing it.

The Eighth Circuit's due process ruling is a judicial articulation of what C4 absence produces: a system that makes adverse determinations about people's lives without a pre-established obligation to explain them. C4 presence would have built that obligation into the operational policy before the first cut was made.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: Arkansas's initial legal position — that the algorithm's methodology was proprietary and need not be disclosed — is confirmed C5 suppression in the government benefit determination context. The state asserted that the basis for cutting a disabled adult's home care hours could be withheld as a trade secret. This is structurally equivalent to the COMPAS trade secret mechanism applied to benefit administration rather than criminal sentencing.

The Eighth Circuit rejected this position, holding that the constitutional right to meaningful notice of adverse government action cannot be waived by a vendor's proprietary interest. The suppression mechanism — proprietary algorithm as shield against disclosure — is confirmed in the court record as an active position the state took and defended through federal appellate litigation.

tags: L-09 Compliance artifacts substitute for safety
laundering.desc: L-06 (Blame shifted onto tools): Arkansas's position was effectively that the algorithm determined the hours — deflecting accountability from the state officials who chose, procured, and deployed the algorithm. L-09 (Compliance artifacts): The procurement process and vendor contract were cited as evidence of due diligence, substituting for actual validation of whether the algorithm produced accurate outputs. L-16: Recipients placed the entire burden of challenging the determination using an appeals process that provided no basis for challenge.

prevention.title: A named state official accountable for algorithm accuracy before procurement sign-off
prevention.desc: Before Arkansas signed the Optum contract, one named official should have documented accountability for confirming the algorithm would produce accurate and legally compliant outputs for the Arkansas Medicaid population. That person's name on the contract as accountable for accuracy — not just administrative compliance — would have required the accuracy question to be answered before deployment.

prevention.title: Independent validation of accuracy for the Arkansas Medicaid population before deployment
prevention.desc: The algorithm should have been independently tested — by a body independent of Optum — against a sample of Arkansas Medicaid recipients whose actual care needs were known, before it was used to cut anyone's hours. If the algorithm's outputs couldn't be validated against known outcomes, it should not have been deployed to make decisions about disabled adults' home care.

prevention.title: Mandatory tracking of health outcomes for recipients whose hours were cut
prevention.desc: A structured monitoring protocol tracking whether recipients who had hours cut experienced adverse health outcomes — emergency department visits, hospitalisation, injury, death — would have provided a real-time signal of whether the algorithm was accurately assessing care needs. That data, reviewed quarterly against a pre-defined threshold, would have identified systematic inaccuracy early.

prevention.title: A requirement to provide written explanation before any benefit cut takes effect
prevention.desc: The Eighth Circuit's ruling articulated this as a constitutional requirement. C4 would have built it into operational policy before litigation was necessary: no reduction in home care hours takes effect until the state can provide a written explanation, in plain language, of the reasons for the reduction specific to that individual's circumstances.

prevention.title: A statutory prohibition on using proprietary algorithm outputs as the basis for benefit cuts without disclosure
prevention.desc: Arkansas's ability to sustain the proprietary shield through federal appellate litigation demonstrates that C5 suppression in algorithmic benefit administration requires legislative intervention to prevent. A state statute — or federal Medicaid regulation — prohibiting the use of proprietary algorithm outputs to reduce benefits without disclosure of the methodology would have made Arkansas's litigation position legally untenable before the first cut was made.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Direct structural comparator for the proprietary algorithm — trade secret — due process failure pattern. COMPAS in criminal sentencing, Arkansas Medicaid algorithm in benefit administration: same mechanism, different domain. Together they define the scope of the proprietary AI accountability gap in US government decision-making.

comparables.name: Michigan MiDAS
comparables.desc: Closest US domestic domain comparator: automated adverse determination in welfare benefits, burden shifted to recipients, no independent validation before deployment. MiDAS operated in unemployment fraud; Arkansas in Medicaid home care. Together they define the US domestic benefits administration failure pattern.

comparables.name: Netherlands Toeslagenaffaire
comparables.desc: International comparator: different jurisdiction, same structural failure — automated benefit determination without independent validation, suppression confirmed, burden reversed onto recipients. Toeslagenaffaire produced a cabinet resignation; Arkansas Medicaid produced a federal appellate ruling. Together they represent the two strongest accountability outcomes in algorithmic benefits administration.

unitedhealthcare-nh-predict-ai-denial-sy.overviewTitle: A 90% appeal reversal rate. Physicians weren't told the algorithm existed.
unitedhealthcare-nh-predict-ai-denial-sy.overviewNote: UnitedHealthcare's nH Predict AI denied post-acute care coverage for Medicare Advantage patients. 90% of denials overturned on appeal. The physicians treating those patients were never told an AI was making coverage decisions about their patients.
overview: UnitedHealthcare's nH Predict (navAIgate Health Predict) is an artificial intelligence system used to make coverage determinations for post-acute care — skilled nursing facility stays and home health services — for Medicare Advantage enrollees. The system was used to predict how many days of post-acute care a patient would need following a hospitalization, and to generate automatic coverage denials when the algorithm's prediction differed from the treating physician's recommendation. Physicians were not informed that an AI system was making coverage decisions about their patients.

A 2023 investigation by STAT News and a subsequent federal lawsuit filed by the estate of a deceased Medicare Advantage enrollee documented that nH Predict had an appeal overturn rate of approximately 90% — meaning that when patients or their physicians appealed AI-generated denials, the denial was overturned in roughly nine out of ten cases. Despite this documented error rate, UnitedHealthcare continued using the system without modification. The federal lawsuit, filed in 2023, alleges that UnitedHealthcare knowingly used an algorithm it knew to be inaccurate to deny coverage and increase profits. The case is active and is the highest-profile current US litigation involving AI-driven insurance coverage denial.
tapeItems: CONTINUED USE DESPITE KNOWN ERROR RATE

timeline.event: nH Predict deployed for Medicare Advantage coverage determinations
timeline.detail: UnitedHealthcare deploys nH Predict to generate automatic coverage determinations for post-acute care. The system is not disclosed to treating physicians. No independent validation of the algorithm's accuracy for this patient population is commissioned before deployment. No named UnitedHealthcare official is documented as accountable for the accuracy of the AI's coverage determinations.

timeline.event: Systematic denials — patients discharged early against physician judgment
timeline.detail: Medicare Advantage enrollees are denied coverage for post-acute care days recommended by their treating physicians. Many are discharged from skilled nursing facilities before their physicians consider them medically ready. Some experience adverse health outcomes including falls, readmissions, and death following early discharge. Patients and families who appeal have denials overturned at very high rates — but the appeals process requires them to fight a denial they did not know was generated by an algorithm.

timeline.event: STAT News investigation — 90% overturn rate documented
timeline.detail: STAT News publishes an investigation documenting nH Predict's appeal overturn rate of approximately 90%. The investigation reveals that physicians treating Medicare Advantage patients are not informed that AI coverage decisions are being made about their patients. The 90% overturn rate means the algorithm was wrong in nine out of ten cases in which patients had the resources and knowledge to appeal.

timeline.event: Federal class action lawsuit filed
timeline.detail: The estate of a deceased Medicare Advantage enrollee, along with other plaintiffs, files a federal class action lawsuit against UnitedHealthcare. The lawsuit alleges that UnitedHealthcare knowingly used nH Predict to deny medically necessary care and increase profits, despite internal knowledge that the algorithm had a high error rate. The lawsuit is active as of the dataset record date.

timeline.event: Congressional attention — Senate investigation opens
timeline.detail: The Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations opens an investigation into AI-driven coverage denials by Medicare Advantage insurers. UnitedHealthcare and other insurers are subpoenaed for internal documentation about the development, validation, and deployment of AI coverage determination systems. The accountability arc is ongoing and unresolved.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named UnitedHealthcare official is documented as accountable for the accuracy of nH Predict's coverage determinations for Medicare Advantage patients. The system was developed and deployed by UnitedHealthcare's data analytics division without a named clinical or compliance official whose documented role required them to certify that the algorithm's outputs were clinically accurate for this patient population.

The 90% appeal overturn rate was available as an internal metric. No named individual was accountable for reviewing it and acting on it. If a named accountable owner had existed with documented responsibility for monitoring the algorithm's accuracy, the 90% overturn rate would have required them to halt or modify the system within months of deployment.
conditions.quote: A 90% error rate was visible in the appeals data. No named individual was accountable for looking at it.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: nH Predict was not independently validated for clinical accuracy in the Medicare Advantage population before being used to override physician coverage recommendations. The algorithm's training data, validation methodology, and accuracy metrics have not been publicly disclosed. No independent clinical body validated the algorithm's ability to predict appropriate post-acute care duration for the specific patient populations it was applied to.

The 90% appeal overturn rate is the empirical consequence of C2 absence: when a body independent of UnitedHealthcare — the appeals process — reviewed the algorithm's outputs against clinical judgment, it found them wrong 90% of the time. That review should have happened before deployment, not after thousands of patients were denied care.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: The appeal overturn rate provided a real-time, continuously updated signal of nH Predict's accuracy. No structured monitoring mechanism required UnitedHealthcare to review the overturn rate against a pre-defined accuracy threshold and halt or modify the system when that threshold was exceeded.

This is the starkest C3 absence in the healthcare domain: the monitoring signal was quantified, available in real time, and showed a 90% error rate. The system continued operating. C3 absence is not the absence of data — it is the absence of the mechanism that converts data into action.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol defined the appeal overturn rate threshold at which nH Predict would be suspended pending accuracy review. No protocol required UnitedHealthcare to notify treating physicians that their coverage recommendations had been overridden by an AI system. The algorithm operated as the default coverage decision-maker without any pre-established checkpoint at which clinical judgment was required to prevail over algorithmic output.

The absence of C4 means that UnitedHealthcare could continue operating an algorithm with a documented 90% error rate because there was no pre-established obligation to stop. The accountability trigger that would have required stopping — a defined error rate threshold — was never set.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: The non-disclosure of nH Predict to treating physicians is confirmed suppression in the clinical governance context. Physicians have a professional and ethical obligation to advocate for their patients' care. UnitedHealthcare's decision not to disclose that an AI system was overriding their recommendations removed their ability to exercise that obligation. This is not inadvertent non-disclosure — it was a deliberate design decision about what information to share with treating clinicians.

The federal lawsuit alleges that UnitedHealthcare had internal knowledge of nH Predict's high error rate and continued using it without modification. If the lawsuit's allegations are proven, this constitutes active suppression of known adverse performance data — the same pattern as Robodebt's suppression of legal advice and Boeing's suppression of simulator data, applied to healthcare coverage determination.

tags: L-09 Compliance artifacts substitute for safety
L-13 Good intent used to negate outcomes
laundering.desc: L-06 (Blame shifted onto tools): Coverage denials attributed to algorithm output without disclosure that physicians could have challenged the determination if they had known. L-09 (Compliance artifacts): Medicare Advantage regulatory compliance framework cited as evidence of appropriate oversight, substituting for independent clinical validation. L-13 (Good intent): Cost efficiency and care coordination framing used to characterise algorithmic denial as clinical management rather than coverage restriction.

prevention.title: A named clinical officer accountable for the accuracy of AI coverage determinations
prevention.desc: Before nH Predict issued its first coverage denial, a named UnitedHealthcare medical officer should have been documented as clinically accountable for the algorithm's accuracy for the Medicare Advantage population. That person's name on record before deployment would have required the accuracy question to be answered. The 90% overturn rate would have landed on a named individual's desk as their documented responsibility to act on.

prevention.title: Independent clinical validation before use in coverage determination
prevention.desc: An independent clinical body — not UnitedHealthcare's own analytics team — should have validated nH Predict's accuracy for the specific patient populations it was deployed to assess. The validation should have tested the algorithm's outputs against clinical outcomes: did patients whose coverage was approved at the algorithm's recommended level recover as expected? If nH Predict had been independently validated, the 90% error rate would have been visible before deployment.

prevention.title: A mandatory monthly review of appeal overturn rates against a pre-defined accuracy threshold
prevention.desc: If UnitedHealthcare's compliance policy had defined in advance that any month in which the appeal overturn rate for nH Predict determinations exceeded 20% triggers mandatory system review and suspension, the system would have been halted within the first months of operation. The threshold was never set. The 90% rate was available and was not acted upon.

prevention.title: A mandatory notification to treating physicians when AI overrides their coverage recommendation
prevention.desc: Any physician whose post-acute care recommendation is overridden by an algorithmic coverage determination should be notified immediately, with a written explanation of the basis for the override and a clear pathway to clinical peer review. This is not a radical requirement — it is the minimum that clinical governance requires when a machine overrides a physician's medical judgment about a patient's care.

prevention.title: A federal requirement to disclose AI use in Medicare Advantage coverage decisions
prevention.desc: CMS Medicare Advantage regulations should require insurers to disclose to enrollees and treating physicians when AI systems are used in coverage determination, and to publish annual accuracy data — including appeal overturn rates — for all AI coverage determination tools. Non-disclosure of AI use in coverage decisions affecting Medicare beneficiaries should be a regulatory violation, not a design choice.

comparables.name: Arkansas Medicaid Algorithm
comparables.desc: Closest structural and domain comparator: proprietary algorithm generating adverse healthcare coverage determinations, no independent validation, burden shifted to affected individuals to challenge outputs they cannot see. Arkansas in Medicaid home care, UnitedHealthcare in Medicare Advantage post-acute care. Together they define the AI healthcare coverage denial failure pattern in US federal health programs.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator for the proprietary algorithm — high error rate — continued deployment pattern. COMPAS in criminal sentencing, nH Predict in healthcare coverage: same structural failure, same mechanism for avoiding accountability, same absence of pre-deployment validation.

comparables.name: Australia Robodebt
tags: Continued operation after error rate known
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator for continued operation after internal knowledge of error rate. Robodebt continued for two years after legal advice confirming unlawfulness was received. nH Predict continued with a documented 90% error rate. The mechanism is different — legal advice vs appeal data — but the institutional behaviour pattern is identical.

detroit-pd-facial-recognition-williams.overviewTitle: Robert Williams was arrested in his driveway because an algorithm said so
detroit-pd-facial-recognition-williams.overviewNote: Detroit Police arrested Robert Williams based solely on a facial recognition match. No corroborating evidence. No human review of the match quality. The technology misidentified a Black man. It is the most documented wrongful arrest by facial recognition AI in the United States.
overview: On January 9, 2020, Robert Williams, a Black man, was arrested in front of his family at his home in suburban Detroit based on a facial recognition match generated by the Detroit Police Department. The match was made by the Michigan State Police's facial recognition system, which compared a still image from a surveillance video of a shoplifting incident at a Shinola store against a driver's license database. The system returned Williams as the top match. A Detroit Police detective confirmed the match by comparing the surveillance still to Williams's driver's license photo — a comparison that does not constitute independent validation. No other evidence connected Williams to the crime.

Williams was held for 30 hours, questioned, and then released. During questioning, when shown the surveillance still and his driver's license photo side by side, Williams held them up and said: 'I hope you don't think all Black men look alike.' He was charged with larceny. The charges were eventually dismissed. The Williams case is the first publicly documented wrongful arrest attributable to facial recognition AI in the United States and is the most-cited case in US legislative and policy debates about facial recognition in law enforcement. The Detroit Police Department subsequently acknowledged that facial recognition matches should not be used as the sole basis for arrest — an acknowledgment that confirmed the department had no such policy in place when Williams was arrested.

timeline.event: Detroit PD adopts facial recognition — no accuracy policy for arrests
timeline.detail: Detroit Police Department begins using the Michigan State Police facial recognition system. No departmental policy defines the accuracy threshold required before a facial recognition match can be used as the basis for arrest. No named official is documented as accountable for ensuring the technology is accurate for the demographic populations it will be used against before deployment.

timeline.event: Shoplifting at Shinola — surveillance footage obtained
timeline.detail: A shoplifting incident at a Shinola store in Detroit is captured on surveillance video. The footage is of poor quality. A still image is extracted and submitted to the Michigan State Police facial recognition system for comparison against the state driver's license database.

timeline.event: Facial recognition match returned — Williams identified
timeline.detail: The facial recognition system returns Robert Williams as the top match. A Detroit Police detective compares the surveillance still to Williams's driver's license photo and confirms the match. This confirmation is not independent validation — it is one officer comparing two photographs, one of which was generated by the same process being validated. No other evidence is sought before the decision to arrest is made.

timeline.event: Williams arrested in front of his family — held 30 hours
timeline.detail: Robert Williams is arrested at his home in front of his wife and daughters. He is taken to a Detroit detention facility and held for 30 hours. During interrogation, he is shown the surveillance still alongside his driver's license photo. He holds them up side by side and states: 'I hope you don't think all Black men look alike.' Detectives continue the interrogation. He is charged with larceny and released on a personal bond.

timeline.event: ACLU documents the case — charges dismissed
timeline.detail: The ACLU of Michigan documents Williams's case and publishes an account. The Wayne County Prosecutor's Office dismisses the charges, acknowledging that the facial recognition match was the only evidence and that it was insufficient. Detroit Police subsequently state that facial recognition matches should not be the sole basis for arrest — confirming that no such policy existed when Williams was arrested.

timeline.event: ACLU files complaint — congressional hearings
timeline.detail: The ACLU files a civil rights complaint on Williams's behalf. The case is cited in multiple congressional hearings on facial recognition in law enforcement. Detroit City Council passes an ordinance restricting facial recognition use. Williams testifies before Congress about his experience. The case becomes the primary reference point in US legislative debates about facial recognition governance.

timeline.event: Legislative and policy responses — no federal standard established
timeline.detail: Multiple cities ban or restrict facial recognition use by law enforcement. Several states introduce legislation. No federal standard for accuracy validation, use policy, or accountability framework for facial recognition in law enforcement has been established. The accountability arc at the federal level remains open.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named Detroit Police Department official was documented as accountable for the accuracy of facial recognition matches before they could be used as the basis for arrest. The decision to use a facial recognition match as the sole basis for arrest was made by individual detectives without a named supervisor or compliance officer whose documented role required them to confirm the match met a defined accuracy standard.

The department's post-arrest acknowledgment — that facial recognition matches should not be the sole basis for arrest — is itself evidence of C1 absence. A named accountable owner for facial recognition use policy would have established that standard before the Williams arrest, not in response to it.
conditions.quote: The department acknowledged after the arrest that a match alone shouldn't justify one. No one owned that question before Williams was in handcuffs.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: The facial recognition system used in the Williams case was not independently validated for accuracy across demographic groups — specifically across skin tones — before Detroit Police deployed it for use in criminal investigations. Academic research published before the Williams arrest, including NIST's Face Recognition Vendor Testing and MIT Media Lab's Gender Shades study, had established that facial recognition systems have significantly higher error rates for dark-skinned individuals than for light-skinned individuals.

Detroit Police deployed the system without commissioning an independent accuracy assessment for the demographic composition of Detroit's population. Williams was misidentified. The misidentification was predictable given the known accuracy disparities for darker-skinned individuals in the systems in use at the time.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring mechanism tracked the accuracy of facial recognition matches used in Detroit Police investigations — specifically, the rate at which matches led to identifications that were confirmed versus overturned by subsequent investigation. The Williams case was not the first facial recognition match Detroit Police had acted on. There is no public record of any monitoring of prior match accuracy.

A monitoring system tracking the confirmed-match rate, disaggregated by the demographic characteristics of individuals identified, would have provided a signal about the system's accuracy in practice before the Williams arrest. That monitoring system did not exist.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol defined the corroboration required before a facial recognition match could be the basis for arrest. The operational default — that a match confirmed by a detective's visual comparison could justify arrest — was not a formally adopted policy. It was the absence of any policy.

The department's post-Williams statement that matches should not be the sole basis for arrest defined a protocol in response to harm. C4 would have required that protocol to exist before the first arrest based on facial recognition was made. The Williams arrest demonstrates what C4 absence produces: a man arrested and held for 30 hours on the basis of a technology the department had no governance framework for using.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: The suppression pattern in the Williams case is the non-disclosure of the facial recognition match to the defendant at the time of arrest and interrogation. Williams was not told that he had been identified by facial recognition technology. He was questioned as though the identification were the result of a traditional investigative process. The technology that produced the identification — and its known accuracy limitations — were not disclosed.

The confirmation is in the interrogation record: detectives continued to question Williams after he directly challenged the identification ('I hope you don't think all Black men look alike'), without disclosing that the identification came from a system with documented accuracy disparities for dark-skinned individuals. The non-disclosure was not inadvertent — it was the operational practice.

tags: L-09 Compliance artifacts substitute for safety
laundering.desc: L-06 (Blame shifted onto tools): The detective's visual confirmation of the match created an appearance of human review that substituted for genuine independent validation. L-03: Williams was interrogated without disclosure of the technology's role, framing the identification as the result of traditional investigation. L-09: The existence of a facial recognition program, purchased and operational, was treated as evidence of appropriate capability without any governance framework governing its use.

prevention.title: A named official accountable for facial recognition use policy before deployment
prevention.desc: Before Detroit Police ran its first facial recognition query for a criminal investigation, one named official should have been documented as accountable for confirming the department had a lawful and accurate use policy. That person's name on record as responsible for the policy means the Williams arrest — based on a match with no corroborating evidence — would have required their sign-off on a use that violated their documented responsibility.

prevention.title: Independent accuracy validation for Detroit's population demographics before deployment
prevention.desc: NIST and academic research had established that facial recognition systems have significantly higher error rates for dark-skinned individuals before Detroit deployed this technology. Detroit Police should have commissioned independent accuracy testing specifically for the demographic composition of Detroit before using the system in criminal investigations. If tested, the system's elevated false match rate for dark-skinned individuals would have been quantified and required to meet a defined standard before use.

prevention.title: Mandatory tracking of match accuracy against investigation outcomes
prevention.desc: Every facial recognition match used in a Detroit Police investigation should have been tracked: was the match confirmed by investigation, or overturned? Disaggregated by demographic characteristics of the identified individual. A monitoring database of this kind, reviewed quarterly, would have provided a real-time signal of the system's accuracy in actual use before the Williams arrest.

prevention.title: A written policy requiring corroborating evidence before arrest on a facial recognition match
prevention.desc: Detroit Police's post-Williams acknowledgment that a match alone should not justify arrest was the policy that should have existed from day one. Written, published, with named accountability for enforcement. If this policy had existed before the Williams case, the arresting detective would have faced a documented obligation they could be held to. The policy did not exist. The arrest happened.

prevention.title: A mandatory disclosure requirement — suspects must be told if facial recognition was used
prevention.desc: Williams was interrogated without knowing his identification came from an algorithm with documented accuracy problems. A departmental policy — or statutory requirement — mandating disclosure to any individual that facial recognition technology was used in their identification, and that they have the right to challenge the match quality, would have changed the interrogation dynamic and made the suppression of the technology's role formally impermissible.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Closest structural comparator in the criminal justice domain: algorithmic system used to make decisions affecting individual liberty, not independently validated before deployment, documented racial disparity in outputs, trade secret / non-disclosure mechanism preventing scrutiny. COMPAS in sentencing, facial recognition in identification: same structural failure pattern across the pre-trial process.

comparables.name: Clearview AI
comparables.desc: Direct technology comparator: facial recognition deployed to law enforcement at scale without accuracy validation or governance framework. Clearview is the vendor-side case; Williams is the operational consequence case. Together they define the facial recognition governance failure from supply to harm.

comparables.name: UK Post Office Horizon
tags: Algorithm outputs used as sole evidence
comparables.desc: Cross-domain comparator for algorithm outputs used as the sole evidentiary basis in legal proceedings. Horizon's accounting records used to prosecute 900+ subpostmasters; Williams's facial recognition match used to arrest and charge him. Same structural failure: a system's outputs treated as definitive without independent validation.

metadata.Suppression Confirmed: Yes — Technology role not disclosed to suspect
metadata.Justice Lag: Charges dismissed within months; systemic reform ongoing
metadata.Harm Scale: Wrongful arrest · first documented US facial recognition arrest

clearview-ai-immigration-enforcement-use.overviewTitle: 600 agencies. No accuracy standard. No governance framework. Scraped without consent.
clearview-ai-immigration-enforcement-use.overviewNote: Clearview AI scraped 10+ billion facial images from the internet without consent, sold access to 600+ law enforcement agencies, and was deployed in criminal investigations before any jurisdiction established a governance framework for its use.
overview: Clearview AI is a facial recognition company that assembled a database of over 10 billion facial images scraped from publicly accessible internet sources — social media profiles, news photographs, professional directories — without the consent of the individuals depicted. Beginning in 2019, Clearview sold access to its facial recognition search engine to law enforcement agencies, enabling investigators to search an image against the scraped database and receive potential matches with links to the source pages. By early 2020, more than 600 law enforcement agencies had used Clearview, including federal agencies and local police departments, before any governance framework governing its use had been established by any jurisdiction.

Clearview's accuracy across demographic groups has never been independently validated and published. NIST testing of comparable systems, and academic research on facial recognition accuracy disparities, establishes that systems of this type have higher error rates for dark-skinned individuals, women, and older people. Multiple wrongful arrests have been documented in which Clearview matches were used in investigations. Regulatory actions have been taken in multiple jurisdictions — the FTC, UK ICO, Australian Privacy Commissioner, Canadian privacy authorities — finding that Clearview's data collection was unlawful. The company continues to operate in the United States with active contracts with federal immigration enforcement agencies.

harmStats.label:
Law enforcement agencies
before any governance existed

timeline.event: Clearview assembles 10B+ image database — law enforcement pilots begin
timeline.detail: Clearview AI scrapes publicly accessible facial images from social media, news sites, and other internet sources. The scraping violates the terms of service of every major platform from which images are taken. Law enforcement pilots begin without public disclosure. No independent validation of accuracy across demographic groups is conducted before law enforcement use begins.

timeline.event: 600+ agencies using Clearview — no governance framework anywhere
timeline.detail: More than 600 law enforcement agencies gain access to Clearview before any jurisdiction has established a governance framework governing its use: no accuracy standard, no use policy, no audit trail requirement, no prohibition on using matches as sole basis for investigative action. Federal agencies including ICE begin using Clearview for immigration enforcement.

timeline.event: New York Times investigation — existence of Clearview revealed publicly
timeline.detail: The New York Times publishes an investigation revealing Clearview's existence, its law enforcement customer base, and the scale of its database. This is the first public disclosure. Before this investigation, most of the 600+ agencies using Clearview had not publicly disclosed their use. The investigation triggers regulatory inquiries in multiple jurisdictions.

timeline.event: Platform cease-and-desist orders — Clearview defies them
timeline.detail: Twitter, Facebook, Google, YouTube, and LinkedIn send cease-and-desist letters to Clearview. Clearview's legal position is that scraping publicly accessible images is protected by the First Amendment. The company continues operating and continues adding to its database.

timeline.event: FTC action — Clearview prohibited from selling to most private entities
timeline.detail: The FTC reaches a settlement with Clearview prohibiting the company from selling its facial recognition database to most private sector entities. The settlement does not prohibit sales to law enforcement. Clearview continues operating with law enforcement and government agency customers. No federal standard for accuracy validation or use policy for law enforcement facial recognition has been established.

timeline.event: Wrongful arrests documented — accuracy disparities unquantified
timeline.detail: Multiple wrongful arrests in which Clearview matches played a role are documented, including cases in which Black men were arrested based on Clearview matches. Clearview has not published independent accuracy data disaggregated by demographic characteristics. The harm window remains open as the company continues operating with active law enforcement and immigration enforcement contracts.

conditions.name: Named Accountable Owner
text: No named official at any of the 600+ law enforcement agencies that used Clearview was documented as accountable for the accuracy of Clearview matches before they were used in criminal or immigration investigations. Each agency made an individual procurement or access decision without a named accountability owner whose documented role required them to confirm the technology met an accuracy standard before investigators could use it.

The scale of C1 absence in Clearview is multiplicative: it is not absent in one agency but absent in every agency simultaneously, because no agency established an accountability framework before use. The absence of named accountability at the vendor level is compounded by its absence at every customer agency.
conditions.quote: 600 agencies used this technology before any of them had a policy governing how. No one was named as accountable for what it got wrong.

conditions.name: Independent Validation
text: Clearview AI's accuracy across demographic groups has never been independently validated and published. The company has not submitted its system to NIST Face Recognition Vendor Testing. No law enforcement agency that contracted with Clearview commissioned independent demographic accuracy testing before use. The known accuracy disparities in comparable systems — higher error rates for dark-skinned individuals, women, and older people — were not assessed for Clearview before its deployment to 600+ agencies.

This is the highest-stakes C2 absence in the dataset for the criminal justice domain: a system used in criminal and immigration investigations, with documented accuracy disparities in comparable systems, deployed to hundreds of agencies without a single independent validation of whether its outputs meet the accuracy standard required for law enforcement use.

conditions.name: Structured Monitoring
text: No structured monitoring mechanism tracked the accuracy of Clearview matches across the 600+ agencies using it. No aggregated data exists on the number of Clearview-assisted investigations, the confirmation rate of matches, or the demographic characteristics of individuals falsely identified. The wrongful arrests that have been publicly documented were identified through individual litigation and journalism, not through any systematic monitoring process.

The absence of monitoring at scale is itself a governance choice: deploying a technology to 600+ agencies without a monitoring requirement means the error rate in practice is permanently invisible. The harm that is visible is the minimum — only cases where the wrongful identification was discovered and documented. The full error rate is unknown and, without C3, unknowable.

conditions.name: Pre-Established Accountability Protocol
text: No pre-established protocol defined what corroboration was required before a Clearview match could be used as a basis for investigative action in any of the 600+ agencies using the technology. The operational default — that a match is a lead that can be acted on — was not a formally adopted policy with named accountability. It was the absence of any policy.

The consequence of C4 absence at scale is that each of 600+ agencies developed its own informal practice, without any requirement to document it, validate it, or hold named individuals accountable for it. The variation in practice across agencies is itself a governance failure — some agencies may have developed sound practices informally; others did not; and there is no mechanism to distinguish between them or to enforce a standard.

conditions.name: No Active Suppression
text: The suppression pattern in Clearview is the concealment of the technology's use from the individuals it was used against. Defendants in criminal cases were not told that Clearview had been used to identify them. This was not inadvertent — it was the operational practice across agencies. The New York Times investigation revealed the technology's existence to the public; before that investigation, most individuals identified by Clearview in criminal investigations had no way of knowing the technology had been used.

The confirmed suppression element is the active effort by Clearview to prevent disclosure of its customer list. Clearview refused to identify which agencies had access to its technology and actively resisted disclosure requests. This is a vendor-level suppression of information that would have enabled defendants to challenge their identification — structurally identical to the trade secret mechanism in COMPAS, applied at the vendor level rather than in individual proceedings.

tags: L-11 Unnamed experts invoked as authority
laundering.desc: L-05 (Responsibility shifted across vendors): Clearview treated accuracy as the deploying agency's responsibility; deploying agencies treated accuracy as Clearview's responsibility. No one owned it. L-11 (Unnamed experts): Clearview's marketing materials cited unnamed law enforcement success stories as accuracy evidence, substituting anecdote for independent validation. L-03: Customer list concealment prevented defendants from knowing the technology's role in their identification.

prevention.title: A named agency official accountable for facial recognition accuracy before any investigative use
prevention.desc: Before any agency used Clearview in a criminal or immigration investigation, one named official should have been documented as accountable for confirming the technology met an accuracy standard for the demographic populations it would be used against in that jurisdiction. 600 agencies using a technology with no named accountability owner at any of them is the operational definition of C1 absent at scale.

prevention.title: Mandatory NIST testing submission before law enforcement sale
prevention.desc: Any facial recognition vendor selling to law enforcement should be required to submit their system to NIST Face Recognition Vendor Testing and publish the results — including demographic accuracy data — before their first law enforcement contract. Clearview has never submitted to NIST testing. A pre-market validation requirement would have made this a condition of sale, not a voluntary choice.

prevention.title: A federal mandate for law enforcement agencies to report facial recognition match outcomes
prevention.desc: Every agency using facial recognition in criminal investigations should be required to report, to a federal oversight body, the number of matches used, the number confirmed by independent evidence, and the demographic characteristics of individuals identified. Aggregated and published annually. This creates the monitoring baseline that currently does not exist across 600+ agencies using technology with unknown real-world accuracy.

prevention.title: A federal standard prohibiting arrest based solely on a facial recognition match
prevention.desc: The post-Williams acknowledgments from individual departments that a match alone is insufficient should be federal policy, not department-by-department informal practice. A federal standard — codified in DOJ guidance or statute — defining the corroboration required before a facial recognition match can be used as the basis for arrest would have prevented every documented wrongful arrest in this dataset.

prevention.title: A mandatory disclosure requirement in criminal proceedings when facial recognition was used
prevention.desc: Any defendant in a criminal proceeding in which facial recognition contributed to their identification should be entitled to disclosure of that fact, the technology used, the match quality score, and the vendor's published accuracy data for their demographic group. This is a Brady material disclosure question — exculpatory or impeachment evidence relevant to the reliability of the identification — and should be treated as such in every jurisdiction.

comparables.name: Detroit PD Facial Recognition (Williams)
comparables.desc: Direct operational comparator: the Williams case is what Clearview deployment produces at the individual case level. Clearview is the vendor-side case; Williams is the operational consequence. Together they define the facial recognition governance failure from supply to individual harm.

comparables.name: COMPAS Risk Assessment
comparables.desc: Structural comparator for proprietary algorithm deployment in criminal justice without independent validation and with active suppression of accuracy information. COMPAS and Clearview represent the two dominant proprietary AI governance failures in US criminal justice — one in sentencing, one in identification.

comparables.name: PredPol / Geolitica
comparables.desc: Companion case in the criminal justice AI governance failure cluster: predictive policing deployed to 150+ cities without independent validation of bias, discontinued after LAPD audit found bias amplification. Clearview, Williams, and PredPol together define the three failure modes in law enforcement AI: identification, prediction, and deployment at scale.
